filer: Invenergy Wind Development North America LLCInvenergy Solar Development North America LLCInvenergy Thermal Development Holdings LLC ,
source_text: sources/comments/files/20251121-5414__Invenergy-Wind-Development-North-America-LLCInvene/2025 1121 Invenergy DOE LLI ANOPR Comments RM26-4.txt
overall_summary: Invenergy, a large independent power producer, supports DOE's effort to standardize large load interconnection rules while urging FERC to maintain resource neutrality and cost-causation principles. The filing calls for a single combined queue for load and generation, deposits tied to actual network upgrade costs, and hybrid projects studied as one unit. Invenergy opposes arbitrary security demands and extraneous application requirements that favor incumbents over competitive new entrants.

quotes.text: transmission owners across the country have adopted a hodge-podge of load interconnection procedures that often impose significant regulatory and financial burdens that threaten to stymie data center deployment and which to date have not served as an effective mechanism for screening non-speculative interconnection requests, reducing ratepayer costs, or increasing speed to power.
quotes.concern: Disparate, inconsistent rules across jurisdictions fail to filter speculation and burden developers

quotes.text: the existence of disparate rules in each jurisdiction incentivizes large load customers to forum shop for the best rules by submitting interconnection requests in multiple service territories, thereby inducing speculative interconnection requests and increasing systemwide costs.
quotes.concern: Inconsistent rules drive forum shopping and inflate system-wide costs

quotes.text: transmission owners too often require developers to post billions of dollars in security up front to interconnect hybrid facilities without any regard to the actual cost of interconnecting and serving data center load, in some instances requiring hybrid projects to securitize both the load and generation interconnection requests.
quotes.concern: Arbitrary security demands on hybrid projects are untethered to actual interconnection costs

quotes.text: Load and hybrid interconnection requests should be studied in the existing generator interconnection queue(s) with generator interconnection requests and not in a separate large load queue.
quotes.concern: Invenergy asks for a single combined queue rather than a separate large load queue

quotes.text: studying load and generation separately can, and often does, result in the identification of network upgrades that would not be needed had the facilities been studied together.
quotes.concern: Separate queues produce unnecessary network upgrade costs

quotes.text: load interconnections, unlike generator interconnection requests, are studied exclusively by each transmission owner and typically only include transmission solutions in that particular transmission owner's footprint, the study process often overlooks potentially less-expensive transmission solutions that would have been identified by looking at the system more holistically
quotes.concern: TO-only study scope misses cheaper regional transmission solutions

quotes.text: The Commission should also clarify that high voltage, direct current ("HVDC") solutions should be treated as equivalent to co-located generation in the integrated study process, including any expedited study process for co-located (or hybrid) projects.
quotes.concern: HVDC converter stations should qualify as co-located generation for expedited study

quotes.text: the Commission could issue rules creating an expedited interconnection process for load that has contracted with a generator.
quotes.concern: Interim expedited path for loads that already have a generator contract

quotes.text: Invenergy urges the Commission, while it undertakes these reforms, to adopt a transition process that does not prejudice existing large load interconnection customers that have already spent time and committed resources navigating existing state interconnection processes.
quotes.concern: Transition rules should protect customers already deep in existing processes

quotes.text: study deposits for large load and hybrid facilities should be based on the size of the request. For hybrid interconnection requests, there should be only one study deposit that is based on the greater of the generation size or load size, but not both.
quotes.concern: Hybrid projects should pay one deposit sized to the larger component, not both

quotes.text: Any commercial readiness or security required should be determined based on the amount of the network upgrades required to interconnect the load, consistent with the generator interconnection rules and the Commission's cost causation policy.
quotes.concern: Security must be sized to actual network upgrade costs, not arbitrarily set

quotes.text: Invenergy recommends that the Commission require transmission providers to produce transparent, publicly accessible, and timely load forecast information that distinguishes between confirmed and speculative load.
quotes.concern: Public load forecasts should separate confirmed from speculative requests to guide generation siting

quotes.text: load serving entities often require detailed specifications for data center projects at the time of application including ramp rate schedules, planned total investment, number of jobs created, water usage, and local ordinance approvals, among other detailed information about planned facilities. This information is not often available to generation and data center developers at the outset of the interconnection process and bears no relevance to studying the interconnection
quotes.concern: Extraneous application requirements (jobs, water usage, ramp rates) are irrelevant to interconnection study and create barriers

quotes.text: Invenergy agrees with DOE's proposal to make load and hybrid facilities responsible for 100% of the network upgrades that they are assigned through any interconnection studies, rather than allocate such costs to non-large load customers or generators in the interconnection queue.
quotes.concern: Large loads should bear 100% of the network upgrade costs they cause

quotes.text: hybrid projects should be studied as one project for purposes of assigning network upgrade costs, rather than studying the load and generation separately.
quotes.concern: Hybrid facilities should be studied as a unit for cost allocation purposes

bins.name: Disparate Rules Drive Forum Shopping
bins.description: Invenergy argues that the current patchwork of inconsistent rules across jurisdictions pushes large load customers to file interconnection requests in multiple territories to find favorable terms. This speculation inflates system-wide costs and undermines the orderly queue. Standardized pro forma procedures would eliminate the incentive to shop jurisdictions.

bins.name: Single Combined Queue Required
bins.description: Invenergy supports studying load, hybrid facilities, and generation in one interconnection queue rather than separate tracks. Separate study produces unnecessary network upgrade identifications, overlooks cheaper regional solutions, and deprives generation developers of useful load timing information. A common queue would match generation to load and reduce ratepayer costs.

bins.name: Holistic Study Process
bins.description: Invenergy backs a joint study process because separate queues and TO-only footprint analysis miss cheaper regional transmission solutions and generate avoidable upgrade costs. A single queue increases transparency for all developers and limits opportunities for undue discrimination.

bins.name: Cost-Causation-Tied Security and Allocation
bins.description: Invenergy argues that security deposits, study deposits, and network upgrade cost allocation must follow cost-causation principles. Arbitrary multi-billion-dollar security demands untethered to actual upgrade costs block viable projects and favor incumbents. Right-sized deposits would free capital for generation and load development.

bins.name: Hybrid Projects Treated as One Unit
bins.description: Invenergy asks FERC to study hybrid (co-located load and generation) projects as a single entity rather than splitting them into separate load and generation assessments. One combined deposit and one cost-allocation study avoids double-counting security and correctly reflects the net grid impact. HVDC converter stations should qualify for the same expedited co-located treatment as generation.

bins.name: HVDC Treated as Co-located Generation
bins.description: Invenergy urges FERC to clarify that HVDC converter stations qualify as co-located generation in the integrated study process, including any expedited path for hybrid projects. HVDC provides controllable injection, reactive power support, and ancillary services comparable to generation, so excluding it would narrow the set of least-cost solutions.

bins.name: Interim Expedited Path for Contracted Load
bins.description: Pending a comprehensive combined-queue regime, Invenergy proposes an expedited interconnection process for loads that have already contracted with a generator. This interim path provides a prompt avenue for ready projects while FERC develops holistic rules.

bins.name: Interim Flexibility for Ready Projects
bins.description: Invenergy supports building in flexibility during the transition period, including an expedited track for loads with generator contracts, so that projects already prepared to move forward are not held up by the development of broader rules.

bins.name: Protect Customers in Existing Processes
bins.description: Invenergy asks that any transition to new interconnection rules not penalize large load customers that have already invested time and money navigating existing state processes. Transition rules should grandfather or accommodate those with prior commitments.

bins.name: Standardize and Right-Size Deposits
bins.description: Invenergy supports standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties sized to actual project and network upgrade costs. For hybrids, a single deposit based on the larger of the load or generation size prevents double-counting. Extraneous application data requirements like ramp rates, job counts, and water usage add no interconnection value and should be prohibited.

bins.name: Public Load Forecast Transparency
bins.description: Invenergy recommends FERC require transmission providers to publish transparent, timely load forecasts distinguishing confirmed from speculative load. Reliable public forecasts would help generation developers site resources near real demand and improve capacity market accuracy.

bins.name: Prohibit Extraneous Application Requirements
bins.description: Invenergy opposes utility requirements that demand detailed project specifications at application (ramp rate schedules, investment amounts, jobs created, water usage) that are irrelevant to the interconnection study and not imposed on generation applicants. These requirements create barriers to entry and improperly favor load serving entities.

bins.name: Load Bears 100% of Caused Upgrade Costs
bins.description: Invenergy agrees that large load and hybrid facilities should be allocated 100% of the network upgrade costs they cause, consistent with the cost-causation principle. Hybrid projects should be studied as one unit by default so that cost allocation reflects the net grid impact of co-located load and generation.

bins.name: Holistic Regional Transmission Analysis
bins.description: Invenergy points out that TO-only load studies miss cheaper transmission solutions visible only through a broader regional lens, as used in generator interconnection studies. A holistic approach reduces unnecessary overbuilding and keeps costs off non-large-load ratepayers.
